edges.quote: The death of a Kingsguard knight was greatly troubling to Ser Tyland, though Unwin Peake discounted Ser Damon Darry’s talk of sorcery and dragons and put down the death of Regis Groves and his men to outlaws.
edges.note: Regis's death at Harrenhal unsettles Hand Tyland Lannister, though Peake shrugs it off as ordinary outlawry.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): the killing troubles Tyland but produces no further organized action within this unit (the planned larger assault is preempted by the Winter Fever), so a character-disposition target is the honest edge rather than inventing a follow-on decision-event.

edges.quote: Lord Corlys lay in state beneath the Iron Throne for seven days. Afterward his remains were carried back to Driftmark aboard the Mermaid’s Kiss, captained by Marilda of Hull with her son Alyn.
edges.note: Corlys's death is immediately followed by the customary state viewing and funeral voyage to Driftmark.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: funeral rites are the mandated next beat after a great lord's death, not a contested decision.

edges.quote: The baseborn Alyn of Hull, now Alyn Velaryon, had been the Sea Snake's chosen heir, but his succession was not uncontested.
edges.note: Corlys's death opens the Driftmark succession that the disinherited 'silent five' cousins contest by force.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: the death only creates the vacancy/dispute; the cousins' free decision to 'argue with swords' rather than accept the council's ruling is what actually produces the assassination attempt (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: Lord Corlys's seat upon the council of regents had already been offered to an older and more seasoned man: Unwin Peake, Lord of Starpike, Lord of Dunstonbury, Lord of
edges.note: Corlys's death vacates the regency seat that the surviving regents offer to Unwin Peake — the opening rung of his rise to power. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: the death only creates the vacancy; the other regents' free choice to select Peake (over, e.g., the sixteen-year-old Alyn) is the actual appointing act.

edges.quote: Only three remained in King's Landing: Lord Peake, Lord Mooton, and Grand Maester Munkun, who came rushing back inside the Red Keep the moment Ser Robert Darklyn commanded that its gates be opened once again.
edges.note: Peake's prior seat on the council is what puts him among the handful of regents present in the city when Tyland dies, positioning him to seize the Handship.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: being a sitting regent is the precondition for Peake's presence and standing; his (and Munkun's) free decision to set aside the king's appointments and claim the combined office is the actual causing act.

edges.quote: The king had lost all four of his own brothers, then watched his uncle feed his mother to a dragon.
edges.note: Munkun ties Aegon's childhood trauma to a lasting disposition: 'he does not display any outward signs of anger or fear, save in regards to dragons, the very mention of which sends him into a rare rage' (same unit, line 137).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): the traumatic death is the origin of a standing psychological trait, not a discrete decision-event, so the character-target form applies rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: In 133 AC, the new Hand decreed that it should be a day when those who had previously been judged would at last be punished for their crimes.
edges.note: Only once Peake has combined the Handship and Protectorship in himself does he have the authority to empty the dungeons and stage the mass punishment day.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: holding the consolidated office makes the Feast possible, but decreeing it is Peake's own free decision to demonstrate his 'strength and rectitude.'

edges.quote: His leal subjects in the west had long been troubled by reavers from the Iron Islands, the young king declared, and who better to bring peace to the Sunset Sea than his new admiral?
edges.note: Fresh off his Stepstones victory and new admiralty, Alyn is immediately volunteered (through the king's mouth, Unwin's words) to sail against the ironmen.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the decision-event: Alyn's triumph and new office are what make him the obvious pretext, but sending him is Peake's own calculated decision (see I-E10).

edges.quote: The trap was neatly set.
edges.note: Gyldayn frames the ironmen mission as a deliberate trap by Peake — win or lose, it either breaks House Velaryon's power or rids the west of the Greyjoys, and either way removes the insolent Oakenfist from court.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Peake's enmity for Alyn drives the decision to dispatch him on a perilous mission; tier-2/disputed since the 'trap' characterization is the chronicler's interpretive judgment, not a stated fact on record.

edges.quote: If indeed Jaehaera Targaryen was murdered (and there is no shred of proof of that), it was surely done at the behest of the only truly plausible culprit: Unwin Peake, Lord Regent, Lord of Starpike, Lord of Dunstonbury, Lord of Whitegrove, Protector of the Realm, and Hand of the King.
edges.note: Gyldayn names Peake as the only plausible mastermind if Jaehaera's death was murder, though he stresses there is no proof.
edges.rationale: SUSPECTED_OF is capped tier-2 per the vocabulary; this is exactly that pattern — an actor implicated by motive and opportunity, never proven.

edges.quote: Were the king to sire an heir of his own body, that calamity might be averted...but before that could happen, Jaehaera had to be removed so Aegon could remarry.
edges.note: Gyldayn's stated logic is exactly the seed chain: Jaehaera removed -> king freed to remarry -> Peake's succession scheme (the Myrielle betrothal, seven days after the funeral).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): tier-2/disputed because this reasoning sits inside the same hedged 'if murdered' passage as I-E11 — Gyldayn is laying out Peake's presumed motive, not asserting the causation as settled fact.

edges.quote: Some suggested that Lady Rhaena’s miscarriage might have made her so distraught she did not wish to live.
edges.note: One of several competing rumors for Jaehaera's despair: Rhaena's lost pregnancy is said to have driven the little queen to grief.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): explicitly one hedged rumor among several ('Some suggested... Others... countered'), so tier-2/disputed/unattributed rather than a firm claim.

edges.quote: in the order of succession...but Lady Rhaena’s pregnancy had ended in miscarriage, which left only the child growing inside Lady Baela on Driftmark.
edges.note: With Rhaena's pregnancy lost, only Baela's unborn child stands between the crown and 'the whelp of a wanton and a bastard' in Peake's eyes, sharpening his urgency over the succession.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition): the narrowed succession field is what Gyldayn cites as driving Peake's alarm and, by extension, his push to see the king remarried.

edges.quote: Unwin Peake had overreached himself. Thaddeus Rowan and Manfryd Mooton were outraged that he had not seen fit to consult them; matters of such import rightly belonged to the council of regents.
edges.note: Peake's unilateral Myrielle betrothal announcement provokes immediate, open protest from his fellow regents and great lords across the realm.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the backlash is the direct, undeliberated reaction to the betrothal announcement, narrated in the very next beat.

edges.quote: In the face of so much tumult, Lord Unwin was forced to think again. Though he remained determined to wed his daughter Myrielle to the king, he had to do so in a way that would not provoke the lords whose support he needed. Bowing to the inevitable, he mounted the Iron Throne and said, “For the good of his people, His Grace must take another wife
edges.note: The realm-wide backlash against the private Myrielle match is what produces Peake's public fallback: a Maiden's Day ball where the king ostensibly chooses for himself.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text's own 'forced to think again... bowing to the inevitable' language marks this as real causation rather than a mere precondition (ENABLES) — the opposition leaves Peake no real alternative but to stage the ball.

edges.quote: wiser in the ways of power saw unseen hands at work and held their tongues. Were the Hand and his minions responsible for these tragedies and misfortunes, or were they happenstance? In the end it would not matter.
edges.note: A run of injuries and deaths among Myrielle's rival brides ahead of the ball is popularly blamed on Peake and his Fingers, though never proven.
edges.rationale: SUSPECTED_OF (capped tier-2): Gyldayn explicitly declines to resolve the question ('it would not matter'), so this is suspicion, not established causation — the disputed/suspicion register the assignment calls for.

edges.quote: “Brother,” Lady Rhaena said to Aegon, “if it please you,
edges.note: Rhaena (with Baela) rides into the throne room and presents Daenaera Velaryon to the king, breaking Peake's scheme. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Role edge completing the ball hub with the marquee pivot moment the assignment calls out — 'the twins present Daenaera.'

edges.quote: As she stood before the king that Maiden’s Day, clad in pale white silk, Myrish lace, and pearls, her long hair shining in the torchlight and her cheeks flush with excitement, Daenaera was but six years old, yet so beautiful she took the breath away.
edges.note: Daenaera's presentation before the king is the ball's decisive moment.
edges.rationale: Role edge (ATTENDS) completing the ball hub for the chosen bride.

edges.quote: Caught in a snare of his own making, Lord Unwin Peake had no choice but to accept the king's decision with as much grace as he could muster.
edges.note: Aegon's surprise choice of the six-year-old Daenaera over Myrielle leaves Peake humiliated and enraged — 'the king's choice undoes Peake's scheme.'
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition): the ball's outcome doesn't force a single further act within this unit, but it curdles Peake's animus toward the king and the twins, the disposition that drives his later conduct.

edges.quote: The Hand declined quickly after that. The fever took his life in two days instead of the usual four.
edges.note: Tyland Lannister, already exhausted from tending the epidemic, succumbs to the Winter Fever himself.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plain disease causation with no human decision mediating it.

edges.quote: Lord Westerling and Lord Mooton both grew ill. Lord Mooton’s fever broke and he survived, though much weakened. Roland Westerling, an older man, perished.
edges.note: Regent Roland Westerling dies of the Winter Fever, unlike Mooton, who recovers.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plain disease causation.

edges.quote: The Dowager Queen Alicent of House Hightower, second wife of King Viserys | and mother to his sons, Aegon, Aemond, and Daeron, and his daughter Helaena, died on the same night as Lord Westerling, after confessing her sins to her septa.
edges.note: Alicent Hightower dies of the Winter Fever the same night as Lord Westerling.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plain disease causation (verbatim source has an OCR-glitched 'Viserys |' for 'Viserys I', copied exactly per the quoting rule).

edges.quote: Widespread famine was reported in the North, and the Winter Fever descended on Barrowton,
edges.note: The ongoing epidemic finally reaches Barrowton, the first time it had traveled so far inland.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: straightforward continuation/spread of the same disease into a new location; no umbrella outbreak event node exists project-wide to route this through instead (concept.medical node is the correct anchor).

new_nodes.name: Death of Regis Groves
new_nodes.identity: Ser Regis Groves of the Kingsguard, sent to reclaim Harrenhal from the self-styled 'witch queen' Alys Rivers, is killed before her gates within an instant of refusing to kneel to her infant son; chroniclers dispute whether by sorcery, crossbow, or sling.

new_nodes.name: Unwin Peake named to the council of regents
new_nodes.identity: Following Lord Corlys Velaryon's death, the surviving regents offer his vacated council seat to Unwin Peake of Starpike — previously excluded from the original seven regents and known to have been angered by the snub.

new_nodes.name: Death of Jaehaera Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Queen Jaehaera, ten years old and the last surviving child of Aegon II, dies on the twenty-second day of the ninth moon of 133 AC after falling (or being thrown) from a window in Maegor's Holdfast onto the spikes of the dry moat below — officially ruled a suicide like her mother Helaena's death, but widely suspected as murder at Unwin Peake's instigation.

new_nodes.name: Opposition to the Myrielle betrothal
new_nodes.identity: Lords Rowan, Mooton, Tully, and Blackwood, Lady Arryn, Cregan Stark, and a wavering Grand Maester Munkun all protest Unwin Peake's unconsulted betrothal of his daughter Myrielle to the newly-widowed King Aegon III, forcing Peake to abandon a private match for a public Maiden's Day ball.

new_nodes.name: Maiden's Day curse
new_nodes.identity: A rash of injuries, scandals, and deaths befalling highborn maidens considered rivals to Myrielle Peake in the months before the Maiden's Day Ball; smallfolk spoke of a curse, while the shrewd suspected Unwin Peake's hand, though nothing was ever proven.

flags: Thaddeus Rowan arc (seed rows 193/194) is explicitly unit 24's territory per the assignment note — left untouched here.

Minted 5 new nodes after confirming via Glob/Grep that no existing node covers death-of-regis-groves, unwin-peake-named-to-council-of-regents, death-of-jaehaera-targaryen, opposition-to-the-myrielle-betrothal, or maidens-day-curse — each is a genuine chain anchor the seeds/marquee-spine note require and none is an umbrella/arc parent (each is a discrete narrated beat with its own scene).
maidens-day-ball and regency-of-aegon-iii are both mistyped 'event.battle' from the wiki-ingestion pass (neither is a battle) — not fixed here, flagged for a future schema-drift pass.

winter-fever (graph/nodes/medical/winter-fever.node.md) is a concept.medical node with zero prior edges; wired it CAUSES into 4 targets (death-of-tyland-lannister, death-of-roland-westerling, death-of-alicent-hightower, winter-fever-reaches-barrowton) per the section note pointing at DIED_OF/AFFLICTED_BY-style plague wiring — used CAUSES from the locked ladder instead of DIED_OF/AFFLICTED_BY since those aren't in the SHARED-RULES locked vocabulary for this pass, and CAUSES cleanly captures unmediated disease causation.
death-of-corlys-velaryon, lady-rhaenas-miscarriage, and winter-fever-reaches-barrowton (the 3 named stubs) each received 2 edges, well past the ≥1 minimum.
Checked graph/edges/edges.jsonl for all 24 source/target pairs before writing — zero pre-existing causal-type collisions found.

harvest.note: Tyland's three fortified granaries (King's Landing, Lannisport, Gulltown) and the resulting grain-price spike that pleased sellers but angered innkeeps and the poor — hospitality/economics texture, not yet extracted.

The Citadel names this period the 'Winter of the Widows' (Archmaester Abelon's *When Women Ruled: Ladies of the Aftermath*) — a named historiographical epoch, not currently a node; possible future concept/era tag.
'Your Grace does not like turnips, but when your cooks prepare them, you eat them, do you not?' — Peake's own line that gives rise to the 'Lady Turnips' nickname; vivid, homeless quote.
Lady Samantha's letter naming 31 candidate brides plus a joking postscript about 'pretty boys' for the king — comedic court-politics texture, homeless quote.

dropped_at_adjudication.reason: character as TRIGGERS source — wrong shape; refusal beat lives in death-of-regis-groves identity
